text:
5 Stoic Principles for Modern Living
Applying an Ancient Philosophy to the 21st Century
Harley Monk

The Greeks put a lot of thought into how life should be lived, from Aristotle to Socrates to Plato, but one of their most practical philosophies remains misunderstood by the majority of people: Stoicism
summary: Stoicism, an ancient Greek philosophy, offers practical principles for modern living, emphasizing resilience, self-control, and rational thinking to navigate lifes challenges effectively.
title: Modern Stoicism: Timeless Principles for Today

text: Despite Stoicism’s undeserved reputation for being synonymous with emotionlessness, it can actually lead to an incredibly satisfying way of life
title: The Misunderstood Joy of Stoicism
summary: Stoicism, often mistakenly viewed as emotionless, can actually foster a deeply fulfilling life.

text: Stoicism is a very old philosophy that can be practiced by anyone to increase their happiness; many ideas you may already practice in your daily interactions
summary: Stoicism is an ancient philosophy accessible to everyone that can enhance happiness, incorporating ideas often practiced in daily interactions.
title: Embracing Stoicism for Greater Happiness

text: Stoicism is, at its root, a philosophy for minimizing the negative emotions in your life and maximizing your gratitude and joy; it includes mindfulness practices and value-based living
title: Stoicism: Cultivating Joy and Gratitude
summary: Stoicism is a philosophy focused on reducing negative emotions while enhancing gratitude and joy, incorporating mindfulness practices and value-based living.

text: Stoicism is a tool to amplify your human experience, both internally and externally
summary: Stoicism enhances the human experience by improving both internal thoughts and external interactions.
title: Amplifying Human Experience Through Stoicism

text: In this article, I’ll share some of the ways that Stoics think by explaining the meaning behind some of their most famous practitioner’s quotes
title: Understanding Stoic Wisdom Through Quotes
summary: The article explores Stoic philosophy by analyzing the meanings of quotes from notable Stoic practitioners.

text: By incorporating some of their thought processes into our daily lives, I believe we’ll find more joy in our daily duties and respond more resiliently to troubles and challenges that arise
summary: Incorporating certain thought processes into our daily lives can enhance our joy in responsibilities and improve our resilience in facing challenges.
title: Finding Joy in Daily Life

text: Principle 1: You can’t change things outside of your control, but you can change your attitude
title: Change Your Attitude
summary: You cannot alter external circumstances, but you can adjust your mindset.

text: “You have power over your mind — not outside events
summary: You control your mind, not external circumstances.
title: Mastering Your Mind

text: Realize this, and you will find strength
title: Discover Your Strength
summary: Recognizing your inner strength leads to empowerment.

text:
”
― Marcus Aurelius, Meditations

A key component of Stoicism is practicing mindfulness
summary: A central aspect of Stoicism is the practice of mindfulness, as emphasized by Marcus Aurelius in his Meditations.
title: Mindfulness in Stoicism

text: Recognize the events in your life that you do and don’t have control over
title: Understanding Control in Life Events
summary: Identify the aspects of your life that you can influence and those that you cannot.

text: If you become frustrated with events outside of your control, you are wasting energy and fostering negative emotion
title: Letting Go of Frustration
summary: Frustration over uncontrollable events drains energy and promotes negativity.

text: The stoic practice of protecting your mind from circumstances out of your control is best illustrated by a Buddhist story
title: Mind Protection: A Stoic and Buddhist Tale
summary: The stoic practice of safeguarding ones mind from uncontrollable circumstances is effectively demonstrated through a Buddhist story.

text: The antagonist in the story is Mara, an enemy of the Buddha
summary: Mara is the antagonist in the story and serves as an enemy of the Buddha.
title: Mara: The Buddhas Antagonist

text: Mara heard of the Buddha’s powers and sought to destroy him so he sent a powerful army
title: Maras Assault on the Buddha
summary: Mara, aware of the Buddhas powers, attempted to eliminate him by sending a formidable army.

text: Mara instructed the soldiers to throw flaming rocks at the Buddha, but when they got near him they turned to flowers and fell
summary: Mara commanded the soldiers to hurl flaming rocks at the Buddha, but as they approached, the rocks transformed into flowers and gently fell instead.
title: From Flames to Flowers

text: Buddha’s adversary then instructed the army to shoot arrows at the Buddha, but again the arrows became flowers once they got close to the Buddha’s sphere
title: Arrows Turned to Flowers
summary: Buddhas adversary commanded the army to shoot arrows at him, but the arrows transformed into flowers as they approached his presence.

text: There was nothing Mara could do to injure the Buddha because the Buddha had mastered the ability to shelter his happiness from outside events
title: Unshakeable Happiness
summary: Mara was unable to harm the Buddha, who had learned to protect his happiness from external influences.

text: I draw parallel the rocks and arrows to negative thoughts about external circumstances
summary: Negative thoughts about external circumstances are compared to rocks and arrows.
title: Rocks, Arrows, and Negative Thoughts

text: You can’t change these events; you can only change your attitude towards them
summary: You cannot alter past events, but you can adjust your attitude towards them.
title: Change Your Attitude

text: Through this realization our mind can become impenetrable
title: Impenetrable Mind
summary: Our mind can become impenetrable through realization.

text: So long as we can control our attitudes and reactions, we can very well never be negatively influenced by outside events
title: Mastering Our Reactions
summary: Controlling our attitudes and reactions allows us to remain unaffected by negative external events.

text: Principle 2: Don’t fall prey to modern society’s materialistic nature
summary: Avoid succumbing to the materialism prevalent in modern society.
title: Resist Materialism

text: “Wealth consists not in having great possessions, but in having few wants
summary: Wealth is defined by having few desires rather than by the accumulation of material possessions.
title: True Wealth

text:
”
― Epictetus

Our consumerist society seems to create more desire than it fulfills
title: Desire in Consumerism
summary: Epictetus highlights that our consumerist society generates more desire than it satisfies.

text: This results in everyone keeping up with the Jones’ — but it’s doubtful that even the Jones’ are happy
summary: Everyone strives to match the perceived happiness of others, yet its uncertain if even those they emulate are truly content.
title: Keeping Up with the Joneses: A False Ideal

text: Our constant exposure to media and advertising keeps us wanting and seeing better out there — we spend our hard earned money on the latest fad, convinced it will leave us fulfilled until version 2
summary: Constant media and advertising exposure drives us to pursue the latest trends, leading us to spend money on fads in the hope of finding fulfillment, only to be left wanting more with each new version.
title: Endless Pursuit of Fulfillment

text: 0 comes out next year
title: 0 Releases Next Year
summary: 0 is set to be released next year.

text: If we strive to want less, our desire decreases and we become more satisfied with what we have, which brings us to the next quote
summary: Striving to want less leads to decreased desire and greater satisfaction with what we have.
title: Finding Satisfaction in Less

text: Principle 3: Picture life without the people and possessions you have to truly appreciate them
summary: Appreciate the value of your people and possessions by envisioning life without them.
title: Appreciate What You Have

text: “Do not indulge in dreams of having what you have not, but reckon up the chief of the blessings you do possess, and then thankfully remember how you would crave for them if they were not yours
title: Gratitude for What You Have
summary: Appreciate and be thankful for the blessings you have instead of longing for what you lack.

text:
”
― Marcus Aurelius, Meditations

We’ve realized now that wanting more leads to dissatisfaction — so how do we find happiness? The key lies in gratitude
summary: Wanting more often leads to dissatisfaction; true happiness can be found through gratitude.
title: The Key to Happiness: Gratitude

text: We must appreciate all that we have and find joy in it
summary: Appreciate and find joy in all that we have.
title: Embrace Joy in Gratitude

text: We live in an incredible period of history with easy access to necessities and technology that provides a standard of living that was unforeseeable just a few generations ago
title: Unprecedented Access in Modern History
summary: We are experiencing an extraordinary era with unprecedented access to necessities and technology, resulting in a standard of living unimaginable just a few generations ago.

text: Instead of appreciating this, we take it for granted
summary: We often fail to appreciate what we have and instead take it for granted.
title: Taking for Granted

text: One stoic practice is to imagine that you lost some of your valuable possessions
summary: A stoic practice involves imagining the loss of valuable possessions to cultivate appreciation and resilience.
title: Imagining Loss: A Stoic Practice

text: It may sound depressing initially, but by imagining these losses we come to appreciate what we have more
title: Embracing Loss to Appreciate Life
summary: Imagining losses can lead to a greater appreciation for what we have.

text: It’s imperative that we not put too much necessity on the things we have either — for they may not always be there
summary: We should avoid overvaluing our possessions, as they may not always be available.
title: Embrace Impermanence

text: Being a Stoic means finding happiness through whatever you do have — if you place emphasis on an external item and it is taken away, the stoic should not be upset but rather grateful they had the object to begin with
summary: Being a Stoic involves finding happiness in what you have and being grateful for it, rather than becoming upset when external possessions are lost.
title: Finding Happiness in Acceptance

text: Everything is borrowed from the universe
summary: Everything in existence is derived from the universe.
title: Borrowed from the Universe

text: Mass comes and goes, but goodness remains, and therein lies joy
summary: Goodness endures beyond fleeting moments, bringing true joy.
title: Enduring Goodness

text: Principle 4: Be genuinely cheerful in all your interactions
summary: Be genuinely cheerful in all your interactions.
title: Cheerful Interactions

text: “A man thus grounded must, whether he wills or not, necessarily be attended by constant cheerfulness and a joy that is deep and issues from deep within, since he finds delight in his own resources, and desires no joys greater than his inner joys
title: Inner Joys of a Grounded Man
summary: A grounded man experiences constant cheerfulness and deep joy from within, finding delight in his own resources and desiring no external joys beyond his inner happiness.

text:
”
― Seneca, The Stoic Philosophy of Seneca: Essays and Letters

We now know that happiness comes from within and from appreciating everything around us — even something as simple as living at a point in time where we can buy bottled water for $1
summary: Happiness originates from within and is enhanced by appreciating the simple pleasures of life, such as the ability to buy bottled water for a dollar.
title: Finding Happiness Within

text: 00 from a vending machine no more than 100 feet from us (We really take things for granted sometimes)
summary: A vending machine nearby reminds us how we often take simple conveniences for granted.
title: Convenience We Overlook

text: Since, with the stoic mindset, our happiness can become independent of other factors; we should be cheerful all the time as stoics because we desire nothing more than human experience
title: Embracing Stoic Cheerfulness
summary: With a stoic mindset, happiness becomes independent of external factors, allowing us to maintain cheerfulness as we embrace the human experience.

text: If we desired more, we could allow ourselves to be disappointed
title: Embracing Disappointment
summary: Desiring more opens the possibility of disappointment.

text: This doesn’t mean that stoics can’t enjoy the finer things in life — it just means we shouldn’t think of them as necessary for our happiness
summary: Stoics can appreciate lifes luxuries but recognize they are not essential for happiness.
title: Stoicism and the Finer Things

text: Only providing goodness to the world through helping people and advancing society — something we each are able to do on a daily basis — can make us truly fulfilled
summary: True fulfillment comes from helping others and advancing society, which we can all do daily.
title: Daily Acts of Goodness

text: Principle 5: Practicing your values beats preaching them
title: Live Your Values
summary: Practicing your values is more impactful than merely preaching them.

text: “Don’t explain your philosophy
summary: Avoid discussing your philosophy directly.
title: Embrace Silence

text: Embody it
title: Embody It
summary: Embody the essence of your message fully and authentically.

text:
”
― Epictetus

Stoic philosophy requires a great deal of personal responsibility
title: Embracing Personal Responsibility in Stoicism
summary: Stoic philosophy emphasizes the importance of personal responsibility in ones life.

text: It’s not that you should be insanely hard on yourself, but you should understand that every decision that you make throughout the day contains a moral dimension
summary: Every decision you make daily carries a moral dimension, so while its important not to be overly hard on yourself, its essential to recognize this aspect of your choices.
title: Mindful Decision-Making

text: Stoicism must be practiced if you want to influence the world in a better way
summary: Practicing Stoicism is essential for positively influencing the world.
title: Practicing Stoicism for Positive Influence

text: Ask yourself regularly, “What’s the best way to act in this situation?” You should be looking to make choices based on your personal values to increase the greater good
title: Acting with Purpose
summary: Regularly consider the best actions in each situation, making choices aligned with your personal values to promote the greater good.

text: Live by your moral code
title: Uphold Your Values
summary: Live according to your principles and values.

text:
Get Harley Monk’s stories in your inbox
Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer
summary: Subscribe to Medium for free to receive updates on Harley Monks stories.
title: Subscribe to Harley Monks Stories

text:
Enter your email
Subscribe
I’ve personally found that the lessons of stoicism, if practiced, can increase our quality of life
title: Embracing Stoicism for a Better Life
summary: Practicing stoicism can enhance our quality of life.

text: Stoicism is not an “all or nothing” philosophy; feel free to choose which parts of stoicism would best impact your life
title: Embrace Stoicism Selectively
summary: Stoicism allows for a selective approach, enabling individuals to adopt the aspects that will most positively influence their lives.

text: To summarize Stoicism, don’t get caught up in materialism, have cheerful interactions with your colleagues to bring them up, perform work diligently and for the greater good, and recognize what’s in and out of your control — don’t let things out of your control affect your joy
summary: Stoicism emphasizes avoiding materialism, fostering positive interactions with colleagues, working diligently for the greater good, and understanding what is within and beyond your control to maintain joy.
title: Embracing Stoicism: Focus on Joy and Diligence

text: When frustrating things happen, breathe, recognize your emotion and the reason for it, and let it pass
title: Breathe and Let Go
summary: When faced with frustration, take a moment to breathe, acknowledge your feelings and their cause, and allow them to fade away.

text: You can’t do anything about it anyway
summary: Theres no point in worrying since you cant change the situation.
title: Acceptance of Inevitability

text: All that you can control is your reaction, and all you can do is embody goodness and appreciate all you have, which is something I’m sure we’ll all find joy in
summary: Embody goodness and appreciate what you have, as your reaction is the only thing you can control, leading to joy.
title: Embodying Goodness and Joy
